title: Endemic amphibian species
subtitle: [Endemic species](#dod:endemic_species) are those known to occur naturally within one country only.
caption:
Endemic amphibian species

[Endemic species](#dod:endemic_species) are those known to occur naturally within one country only.

This choropleth map displays the count of endemic amphibian species per country in 2025, using a logarithmic color scale ranging from "No endemics" to over 300 species. The visualization highlights a distinct concentration of unique biodiversity in specific tropical and subtropical regions, while vast areas of the globe show little to no endemism. The highest values, represented by the darkest blue shade indicating more than 300 species, appear in Brazil, Colombia, and Madagascar. These nations stand apart as the primary reservoirs for amphibians found nowhere else on Earth. Moving down the scale, countries such as Mexico, Peru, Ecuador, India, China, and Australia fall into the 30 to 100 species band, shown in medium blue tones. This group includes large landmasses across the Americas, Asia, and Oceania that host significant but less concentrated unique populations. A lighter teal color marks nations with between 10 and 30 endemic species, covering much of Central America, parts of Southeast Asia including Indonesia and the Philippines, and scattered nations in Africa like Tanzania. The lightest green shades denote countries with between 1 and 10 endemic species, a category that encompasses most of Europe, the United States, Canada, and large portions of Africa and the Middle East. Areas colored in pale beige, such as Russia, Greenland, and the Sahara Desert region, report no endemic species. The geographic distribution shows that endemic amphibian diversity clusters heavily in the Neotropics and parts of South and Southeast Asia, whereas high-latitude regions and arid zones generally lack species restricted to a single national boundary.
